timeline_link.text: See on Timeline — Valley of Dry Bones

sections.header: Verses 1-14 — The Valley of Dry Bones

heb.paragraph: The bones are not merely dead but yeveshoth (dried out), indicating that death occurred long ago. All moisture, marrow, and organic material have vanished. These bones are beyond any natural restoration. The dryness emphasizes the impossibility of the situation: only sovereign divine power can reverse this kind of death.

The word ruach appears ten times in this passage, carrying all three of its possible meanings simultaneously: wind (the physical phenomenon God commands), breath (the life force entering the corpses), and spirit (the divine Spirit animating the restored people). The deliberate ambiguity creates a theological tapestry: God's Spirit operates through the physical medium of breath carried on the wind. This triple meaning echoes Genesis 2:7 and anticipates John 3:8.

Ezekiel is commanded to prophesy twice: once to the bones (v. 4) and once to the ruach (v. 9). The prophetic word itself is the instrument of resurrection. This is not mere prediction but performative speech: the word creates the reality it announces. The prophet's voice becomes the vehicle of divine creative power.

panels.ctx: This is the most famous passage in Ezekiel and one of the most vivid visions in all of Scripture. The hand of the LORD sets Ezekiel down in a valley full of dry bones and asks: 'Can these bones live?' (v. 3). The question is rhetorical — humanly speaking, the answer is no. But Ezekiel defers: 'Sovereign LORD, you alone know.' The vision enacts what chapter 36 promised: God breathing His Spirit into dead Israel to bring them back to life. The primary referent is national restoration from exile (v. 11: 'these bones are the whole house of Israel'), but the imagery has fueled resurrection theology throughout Jewish and Christian tradition. The two-stage restoration — first bodies assembled (vv. 7-8), then breath/spirit given (vv. 9-10) — emphasizes that physical reconstitution without the Spirit is not yet life.

cross.note: God formed Adam from dust and breathed life into him. Ezekiel 37 is a corporate re-creation: God reassembles bodies from bones and breathes His Spirit into them. The creation of humanity is recapitulated in the restoration of Israel.
Paul describes God as the one 'who gives life to the dead and calls into existence the things that do not exist.' This is precisely what Ezekiel 37 enacts: calling dry bones into living people.
The breath of life entering the two witnesses echoes Ezekiel 37, extending the dry bones pattern into eschatological resurrection.
Elijah's resurrection of the widow's son uses similar breath-of-life language. Ezekiel scales the individual miracle to a national scale.

notes.note: The vision opens with a question that tests Ezekiel's theology: can God raise the dead? Ezekiel's answer, 'You alone know,' is the perfect response of faith that acknowledges both human impossibility and divine omnipotence. God never asks rhetorical questions; He asks questions that reveal the heart of the respondent.

The prophetic word itself is the agent of resurrection. This demonstrates the power of God's Word as a creative force (cf. Gen 1; Heb 4:12). The bones do not self-assemble; they respond to the Word spoken through the prophet. The two-stage process (bodies first, breath second) mirrors creation: form, then life.

The command to prophesy to the ruach (breath/wind/spirit) is a command to invoke the Holy Spirit. The four winds echo the four corners of the earth, indicating that God's life-giving power comes from every direction. The result is an exceedingly great army, not merely survivors but a force ready for action.

The interpretation is explicit: the bones represent the whole house of Israel in exile, who say 'Our hope is gone.' This is the ultimate promise of national restoration, pointing toward Israel's future regathering and spiritual renewal in the millennial kingdom. God opening graves is both metaphorical (exile as death) and prophetic (eschatological resurrection).

Calvin reads the vision as God's demonstration that His power is not limited by human impossibility. The drier the bones, the greater the miracle. Calvin applies this to the church in every age: when the situation appears most hopeless, God's power is most dramatically displayed.

Calvin emphasizes the two-stage process as theologically significant: the body without the Spirit is not yet alive. External reformation without internal transformation is as useless as a corpse with skin but no breath. The lesson for the church is that organizational structure without the Spirit is a decorated corpse.

The phrase 'the hand of the LORD was on me' (hayetah alay yad-YHWH) is Ezekiel's standard formula for visionary experience (cf. 1:3; 3:14, 22; 8:1; 33:22; 40:1). It indicates an irresistible divine compulsion, not merely inspiration.

The command to prophesy to the ruach from the 'four winds' (arba ruchot) involves a wordplay: the ruach (spirit/breath) comes from the ruchot (winds). The fourfold direction indicates totality: God's life-giving power is comprehensive.

Block identifies this vision as the climax of Ezekiel's restoration oracles. The three-stage movement (bones assemble, flesh covers, breath enters) recapitulates the creation sequence of Genesis 2:7 on a national scale. Block stresses the corporate referent: this is not individual resurrection theology but national restoration prophecy. The 'whole house of Israel' (v. 11) means both kingdoms, north and south, united and restored. The recognition formula in verse 13 applies the formula to the risen dead themselves: even the restored exiles will come to know God through the experience of being raised.

Zimmerli designates this as a 'demonstration vision' (Demonstrationsvision) whose function is to make visible what is otherwise unimaginable. The vision is not allegory but enacted prophecy: God performs the restoration before Ezekiel's eyes as a guarantee that it will occur in history. Zimmerli stresses the performative character of the prophetic word: the command 'prophesy!' is not merely predictive but causative. The word itself generates the reality. This is the prophet's ultimate function: to speak a word that creates what it announces.

sections.header: Verses 15-28 — The Two Sticks: Reunification of Israel and Judah

heb.paragraph: The word ets can mean tree, wood, or stick. The sign-act uses two sticks (or wooden tablets) representing Judah and Ephraim (the northern kingdom). When joined in the prophet's hand, they become one ets. The ambiguity between 'stick' and 'tree' may be intentional: the divided kingdom is like a split tree that God will graft back together.

The restored united kingdom will have one king: 'my servant David.' This title refers not to the historical David but to the eschatological Davidic ruler, the messianic king who will shepherd the reunited people. The title 'my servant' echoes Isaiah's servant songs and connects the Davidic hope to the suffering servant tradition.

panels.ctx: The two-sticks sign-act complements the dry bones vision by addressing the political dimension of restoration. If the dry bones answer the question 'Can Israel live again?', the two sticks answer 'Can Israel be united again?' The northern kingdom fell to Assyria in 722 BC; the southern kingdom fell to Babylon in 586 BC. Ezekiel envisions their reunification under one Davidic king (vv. 22-24), with one sanctuary (v. 26), one covenant (v. 26), and one God dwelling among them forever (v. 27-28). The passage culminates in the covenant formula: 'I will be their God, and they will be my people' (v. 27), the most fundamental statement of covenantal relationship in the Bible.

cross.note: Isaiah's parallel vision: God will gather the scattered of Israel and Judah, and 'Ephraim's jealousy will vanish.' Both prophets envision north-south reunification as an eschatological event.
Jeremiah's parallel promise: 'In those days the people of Judah will join the people of Israel, and together they will come from a northern land.' The three major prophets converge on reunification.
Jesus declares 'I have other sheep that are not of this sheep pen. I must bring them also.' The gathering of one flock under one shepherd echoes Ezekiel 37:24.
Paul's theology of Jews and Gentiles united in one body through Christ extends the two-sticks pattern to its ultimate scope: all division overcome in Messiah.

notes.note: The two sticks represent the political division that has plagued Israel since Rehoboam's folly (1 Kgs 12). God promises to reverse the split permanently. This will be fulfilled when Christ reigns as the Davidic king in the millennium, gathering all twelve tribes to the land under one kingdom.

The messianic promises here are breathtaking: one king (v. 24), one land (v. 25), one everlasting covenant (v. 26), one sanctuary (v. 26-27). This is the blueprint for the millennial kingdom. The sanctuary in verse 26 anticipates the temple vision of chapters 40-48.

Calvin interprets the reunification spiritually as well as historically: the church is one body despite its visible divisions, and God's ultimate purpose is to gather all His people under one Head. The two sticks becoming one in the prophet's hand shows that unity is achieved through the word of God, not through human diplomacy.

Calvin sees the 'servant David' as Christ, the true Davidic king who reigns over the united people of God. The everlasting covenant is the New Covenant established in Christ's blood. Calvin emphasizes the permanence: this covenant, unlike the Mosaic, cannot be broken because it rests on God's faithfulness, not human performance.

The phrase 'for Ephraim' uses the preposition le with the name of Joseph's dominant son, standing for the entire northern kingdom. The NET translators note that this political shorthand reflects the post-722 BC reality where 'Ephraim' replaced 'Israel' as the standard designation for the lost northern tribes.

The phrase berit shalom (covenant of peace) echoes 34:25 and is a technical term for a comprehensive covenant covering all dimensions of life: security, prosperity, and divine presence. It is 'everlasting' (olam), emphasizing its permanence.

Block identifies the two-sticks oracle as a sign-act that combines performative symbolism with verbal interpretation (vv. 21-28). The sign-act tradition in Ezekiel requires the prophet to embody the message physically before explaining it verbally (cf. chs. 4-5, 12, 24). Block notes that the Davidic messiah is called both 'king' (melek, v. 22) and 'prince' (nasi, v. 25), with nasi being Ezekiel's preferred term in the temple vision (chs. 44-46), possibly to distinguish the messianic ruler from the failed kings of Israel's past.

Zimmerli classifies this as a 'sign-act report with interpretive expansion.' The sign-act itself (vv. 15-17) is brief, but the interpretation (vv. 18-28) expands into a comprehensive eschatological program. Zimmerli identifies the passage's theological structure as concentric: the two sticks become one (unity), under one king (leadership), cleansed from sin (holiness), with an everlasting covenant (permanence), and God's sanctuary among them (presence). The movement is from political unity to divine indwelling, with each layer grounding the next.

themes.note: Sovereignty reaches 10: only God can raise the dead and unite the divided. Mercy and Covenant are both at 9 as the chapter is entirely about undeserved restoration and covenant renewal. Prophecy scores 8 because the prophetic word itself is the instrument of resurrection. Faith rises to 7 as the dry bones vision tests and rewards faith in God's power.

lit.note: Two complementary visions of restoration: the first addresses the question of life (can dead Israel live?), the second addresses the question of unity (can divided Israel be one?). Together they envision a restored, unified, Spirit-filled people under a Davidic king — the most comprehensive restoration vision in the Old Testament.

trans.title: Comparing translations in The Valley of Dry Bones

translations.text: I looked, and tendons and flesh appeared on them and skin covered them, but there was no breath in them.
And I looked, and behold, there were sinews on them, and flesh had come upon them, and skin had covered them. But there was no breath in them.

Then he said to me, “Prophesy to the breath; prophesy, son of man, and say to it, ‘This is what the Sovereign Lord says: Come, breath, from the four winds and breathe into these slain, that they may live.’ ”
Then he said to me, “Prophesy to the breath; prophesy, son of man, and say to the breath, Thus says the Lord GOD: Come from the four winds, O breath, and breathe on these slain, that they may live.”
